text: The surgical team prepares for a laparoscopic right colectomy on a patient with a tattoo marking the location of a recently diagnosed cecal tumor. Three ports are established: a 5mm port in the left upper quadrant, a 10mm umbilical port for the camera, and a 12mm suprapubic port.

The video shows a laparoscopic setup for a right colectomy on a patient with a tattoo marking the location of a recently diagnosed cecal tumor. Three ports are used: a 5mm port in the left upper quadrant, a 10mm umbilical port for the camera, and a 12mm suprapubic port.
The surgeon tents the ileocolic pedicle, carefully undoing folds of the mesentery to expose the true base of the pedicle for clearer visualization.
The surgeon tents the ileocolic pedicle, carefully undoing folds of the mesentery to expose the true base of the pedicle.
The surgeon initiates a medial to lateral dissection beneath the ileocolic pedicle, using laparoscopic instruments to carefully separate the tissue layers.
The surgeon initiates a medial to lateral dissection beneath the ileocolic pedicle, carefully separating the tissue layers in this area.
The duodenum becomes visible in the surgical field, and the surgeon begins to sweep it downward to improve exposure.
The surgeon identifies the duodenum as it comes into view and begins to sweep it downward, clearing the surgical field for further dissection.
The surgeon opens the peritoneum surrounding the ileocolic pedicle, using precise dissection to expose the underlying structures.
The surgeon opens the peritoneum around the ileocolic pedicle, exposing the underlying structures for further dissection.

I think whether or not you do a CME dissection or just a standard high ligation of your vascular pedicles, it's really important to really sweep the duodenum down and to completely isolate your pedicle and clear it of all of its fatty tissue to ensure there's no inadvertent injury of the duodenum or inadvertently not doing a high ligation.

The surgeon isolates the vascular pedicles, carefully sweeping the duodenum downward and clearing the pedicle of fatty tissue to prevent injury to the duodenum and ensure proper ligation.
The surgeon exposes the artery and vein of the ileocolic pedicle, preparing to ligate them separately for enhanced security.
The surgeon exposes the artery and vein beneath the ileocolic pedicle, bringing these vascular structures into clear view during the medial to lateral dissection.
And most of the time, I like to take these separately just for security purposes.
Although I do trust the ligature very much, I will clip often before dividing.

The surgeon identifies the ileocolic vein and proceeds to isolate the ileocolic artery, ensuring clear separation from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon identifies the ileocolic vein and proceeds to isolate the ileocolic artery, ensuring clear separation from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon observes residual lymphatic tissue surrounding the vessel, highlighting the importance of cleaning the area thoroughly before vessel transection to avoid excess tissue involvement.
The surgeon observes residual lymph tissue surrounding the vessel, emphasizing the importance of cleaning off excess tissue to facilitate a clear transection.
The surgeon observes bulky lymph nodes within the transected mesentery and continues the medial dissection, progressively working upwards through the tissue.
Bulky lymph nodes are visible in the transected mesentery as the surgeon continues the medial dissection, progressing upwards.
The surgeon performs medial dissection underneath the mesentery along the expected path of the superior mesenteric vein (SMV), following the visible white line as a guide.
The surgeon navigates along the expected location of the superior mesenteric vein (SMV), preparing to isolate and potentially dissect around this vessel.
The surgeon performs medial dissection underneath the mesentery, following the visible white line along the tissue plane.
The surgeon demonstrates the separation of tissue along a bloodless plane, showing how it can be achieved without sharp dissection as the tissue layers naturally divide.
The surgeon demonstrates the separation of tissue in a bloodless plane, showing how it can be achieved without sharp dissection as the tissue layers easily part.
The surgeon uses three ports to achieve adequate exposure and proceeds to dissect upwards towards the right colic vessels.
The surgeon uses three ports to achieve adequate exposure and proceeds to dissect towards the right colic vessels.

The surgeon identifies the head of the pancreas and begins thinning out the mesentery to isolate the blood vessel, revealing the anatomical structures and facilitating clearer visualization for further dissection.
The head of the pancreas is visible in the surgical field during the ongoing dissection.
The surgeon begins thinning out the mesentery to isolate the blood vessel, focusing on the area where the superior mesenteric vein (SMV) is expected to be.
The surgeon uses a ligature to isolate the blood vessel, mindful of potential incomplete or misfires that could cause bleeding. The vessel is fully isolated following oncologic principles.

Although, again, I do trust the ligature, and you could just go straight across this with the knowledge of where the vessel is with the same oncologic principles. Every once in a while, you will have an incomplete fire or a misfire, and the bleeding is very annoying.
When you fully isolate the vessel, I do think that any residual bleeding is easily more controlled when there's not a lot of fat around.
The surgeon isolates a blood vessel, clips, and divides the artery, followed by clipping and dividing the vein. The surgical field shows minimal residual bleeding due to the reduced fat presence.
The surgeon carefully isolates a blood vessel, clips, and divides the artery, followed by clipping and dividing the vein.

And now from a standpoint of oncologic principles of the sequel tumor, I feel that having done a high ligation of those two vessels is pretty adequate. Although I do think that we should all really try to identify the right branch of the middle colic every time we do a right colectomy.

The surgeon continues the medial dissection over the duodenum towards the hepatic flexure, revealing the right branch of the middle colic artery within a wad of tissue. The margin is being identified, although the artery is not completely isolated.
The surgeon continues the medial dissection over the duodenum and up to the hepatic flexure, bringing the right branch of the middle colic artery into view and identifying the surgical margin.
The surgeon navigates through a wad of tissue containing the right branch of the middle colic artery, without fully isolating it, ensuring adequate space for the procedure.
The surgeon identifies the planned colon margin and places a clip for ease of identification. The omentum is taken to the specimen as part of the right colectomy procedure.
The surgeon identifies the planned colon margin and places a clip for ease of identification during the colectomy procedure.

I do write colectomies. I do take the omentum to the specimen. With the specimen, just the way that I was taught, there are those that believe that there's oncologic benefit to this. I'm not really certain unless there was peritoneal disease, but I do take the omentum to the specimen.
The surgeon splits the omentum at the level of the planned colon margin, preparing to include it with the specimen for potential oncologic benefit.

The surgeon splits the omentum at the level of the planned colon margin, then proceeds to the lateral dissection. The medial dissection is visible, having extended over the pancreas and duodenum, facilitating the current dissection phase.
The surgeon proceeds with the lateral dissection, revealing that the previous medial dissection extended over the pancreas and duodenum, facilitating this part of the procedure.

The surgeon completes the lateral dissection around the cecal attachments using a ligature device, following a full medial dissection. The remaining lateral attachments are taken from an inferior angle, facilitated by the minimal tissue layers.
The surgeon completes the lateral dissection, which is facilitated by the prior full medial dissection, leaving only a few cell layers of tissue to separate.
The surgeon performs a full medial and lateral dissection using a ligature device, manipulating the tissue with precision.
The surgeon approaches the cecal attachments, using an inferior angle to detach the remaining lateral attachments, prioritizing speed over technical precision.

The surgeon completes the lateral attachments and proceeds to dissect the small bowel mesentery. The technique involves taking both leaflets of the mesentery simultaneously, revealing the thick tissue layers as the cecum and terminal ileum are mobilized.
The surgeon completes the dissection of lateral attachments and proceeds to dissect the small bowel mesentery, separating it from surrounding tissues.

The surgeon completes the lateral mobilization of the cecum and terminal ileum, unfolding the mesentery to facilitate easier transection of the small bowel mesentery. The mesentery appears thick, and the technique allows simultaneous handling of both leaflets.

The surgeon completes the final portion of lateral mobilization, unfolding the mesentery to facilitate easier transection of the small bowel mesentery. With both medial and lateral attachments completed, the surgeon prepares to transect the bowel and conducts a perfusion assessment.
The surgeon prepares to transect the bowel after completing the medial and lateral attachments. A perfusion assessment is conducted to ensure adequate blood flow in the areas planned for transection.

The surgeon uses ICG mode to assess perfusion at the planned transection sites of the bowel. The stapler is aligned with the colon and transects it. The small bowel is then transected using the stapler through the suprapubic port, which will be upsized for specimen extraction.

The surgeon uses the ICG mode to align the stapler with the bowel and performs the colon transection. The stapling is conducted through the suprapubic port, which will be upsized at the end of the procedure for specimen extraction.

The surgeon places the completely divided specimen into the pelvis. A stay suture is applied to the stapled end of the colon and the small bowel, approximately seven centimeters proximal, using a suture passer for stabilization. An isoparastaltic anastomosis is performed.

The surgeon places the completely divided specimen into the pelvis. A stay suture is applied to the stapled end of the colon and the small bowel, approximately seven centimeters proximal, using a suture passer for stabilization. An isoparastaltic anastomosis is performed.
The surgeon checks the mesentery for any twisting and proceeds to make a colotomy using a Maryland dissector, followed by an enterotomy.
The surgeon checks the mesentery to ensure there is no twisting, then uses a Maryland dissector to make a colotomy and an enterotomy.
The surgeon carefully staples the bowel through the suprapubic port using a vascular load stapler to create the common channel, ensuring not to penetrate the back wall of the bowel.
The surgeon carefully staples through the suprapubic port using a vascular load stapler to secure the common channel, ensuring not to penetrate the back wall of the bowel during the process.

The surgeon aligns the colon and small bowel to minimize the size of the colotomy or common channel enterotomy. Using a barbed suture, the surgeon begins suturing at the near corner, employing a transition stitch and a baseball suture technique to ensure a secure closure. The barbed suture maintains tension with each cinch, and a second layer is applied for added security.

The surgeon aligns the colon and small bowel to minimize the size of the colotomy or common channel enterotomy. Using a barbed suture, the closure begins at the near corner to ensure a secure and visible closure of the surgical site.
The surgeon cinches the suture after each bite while performing a transition stitch on the anastomosis site between the colon and small bowel.

The surgeon performs a baseball suture technique around the near corner of the anastomosis, sewing away from themselves. The suture is applied to the inner layer without narrowing the lumen at this location.

And as I always do a second layer, I don't mind what this looks like. What I like about the barbed suture is that I know that I'm going to maintain the tension on the suture every time I cinch it down. But it really doesn't matter if you choose to use Vicryl or PDS or a barb suture, as long as you space your bites properly and have good tension.

The surgeon uses a nine-inch V-lock suture to perform a Lembert stitch, ensuring proper spacing and tension of the suture bites. The suture effectively buries the first layer, creating a secure closure.

The surgeon takes a bite out past the corner of the tissue before coming back down, preparing to apply a Lembert stitch. A nine-inch V-lock suture is being used, although a six-inch suture could also be suitable.
The surgeon sutures the tissue layers together, effectively burying the first layer with a second layer of sutures. The suturing progresses towards the near corner, ensuring a secure closure.

The surgeon carefully sutures down to the near corner of the anastomosis, ensuring visibility by opening the area to check the depth. A silk suture is occasionally placed as a target for closure. The V-lock suture is secured with clips using a clip applier, followed by cutting the suture tethering to conclude the operation.

Every once in a while, I'll put a silk suture down there at the start of my closure to make sure that I have a target so that I know when I get to that silk suture, I've gone far enough, but It's not mandatory.
The surgeon concludes the operation by securing the V-lock suture with clips using a clip applier, followed by cutting the suture tethering.
